hero.imageAlt: OA brace supporting an osteoarthritic knee

conditionsHeading.title: What is osteoarthritis, and how does bracing help?
conditionsHeading.lead: OA is wear-and-tear arthritis. The right brace doesn't reverse it — but it can change how you feel every day.

conditions.title: Cartilage wear
conditions.description: Cartilage on the ends of bones thins over time, especially in load-bearing joints like the knee and hip.

conditions.title: Offloading the painful side
conditions.description: Most knee OA affects one compartment. An offloader brace shifts load to the healthy side — reducing pain at the worn area.

conditions.title: Restore daily activity
conditions.description: Reduced pain means more walking, more stairs, more life. Activity itself slows OA progression.

conditions.title: Surgery as a last resort
conditions.description: Many patients meaningfully delay or avoid joint replacement with consistent bracing, physiotherapy, and weight management.

productsHeading.title: Recommended OA bracing
productsHeading.lead: These offloader and OA-specific products are chosen by Avana for their clinical performance and Indian patient fit.

seoContent.title: Understanding osteoarthritis — and what helps
seoContent.intro: OA is the most common joint condition in adults over 50. The good news: there is more you can do than just pain medication.

sections.heading: What causes OA?
sections.body: Cartilage — the smooth covering on the ends of bones — thins with age, injury, or repeated stress. The exposed bone surfaces then grind, causing pain, stiffness, and reduced movement.

sections.heading: How offloader braces work
sections.body: Most knee OA affects one compartment (usually medial). An offloader brace applies a corrective force that shifts load away from the worn compartment — reducing pressure on the painful area and easing pain during activity.

sections.heading: The full OA toolkit
sections.body: Bracing works best as part of a plan: physiotherapy for muscle support, weight management to reduce joint load, activity modification, and pain control. Surgery is one option — not the inevitable one.

seoContent.disclaimer: Educational content. Please consult an orthopaedic specialist for diagnosis and treatment.

faq.question: Will a brace cure my osteoarthritis?
faq.answer: No. OA is progressive and no brace reverses cartilage loss. What a well-fitted offloader brace can do is meaningfully reduce pain, improve walking comfort, and help many patients delay or avoid surgery.

faq.question: How long should I wear my OA brace each day?
faq.answer: Offloader OA braces are typically worn during weight-bearing activities — walking, working, light exercise — and removed when resting. Your specialist will recommend a schedule.

faq.question: Can I exercise while wearing the brace?
faq.answer: Yes. Modern OA braces are designed for activity — walking, cycling, gentle resistance training. Avoid high-impact sport unless your specialist clears it.
